text: Daily dietary carbohydrate intake up to 12 g·kg⁻¹·day⁻¹ supports endurance training adaptations in ultramarathon runners.
Multiple-transportable carbohydrate intake of approximately 90 g·hr⁻¹ during exercise lasting 3 hours or more enhances real-time endurance performance.
Whether carbohydrate intake rates of ~90 g·hr⁻¹ are tolerable during ultramarathon competition is questionable from both practical and gastrointestinal perspectives.
Low-carbohydrate high-fat diets increase total fat oxidation rates during submaximal exercise but are not currently supported as enhancing ultramarathon running performance.
Ultramarathon runners may develop both hypohydration and hyperhydration with exercise-associated hyponatremia, depending on event duration and environmental conditions.
Drinking to thirst is generally sufficient to maintain euhydration and avoid both hypohydration and hyperhydration during ultramarathon events.
A well-practiced and individualized nutrition strategy is required to optimize both training and competition performance in ultramarathon running, whether single-stage or multistage events.
